text.title: Problem Validation Framework
text.summary: Evidence hierarchy, Mom Test question rewrites, commitment signal taxonomy, and red flag patterns.

rule.statement: Rank evidence by tier before synthesizing: 1-paid (strongest), 2-committed (LOI/pre-order), 3-engaged (prototype use, 3+ return visits), 4-stated (expressed interest in interview), 5-anecdote (single mention). Only tiers 1-3 justify PROCEED.
rule.rationale: Stated interest is insufficient for a build decision; people are optimistic about their future selves. Only behavioral evidence (paid, committed, engaged) predicts actual adoption.

rule.statement: Replace hypothetical and leading questions with past-behavior and present-reality questions: "Would you use this?" becomes "How do you currently solve X?"; "Is this a problem?" becomes "Tell me about the last time you dealt with X"; "Would you pay?" becomes "What have you tried? What did it cost?"
rule.rationale: Hypothetical questions produce socially desirable answers. Past-behavior questions produce facts that can be verified and compared across interviewees.

example.note: "Would you use this app?" — hypothetical, primes a yes response regardless of actual intent.
"Walk me through how you handle [task] today. What did you try last time it went wrong?" — anchors to past behavior, not future speculation.

rule.statement: After each validation interview, classify evidence by signal type: Time (scheduled follow-up, unprompted prototype use), Reputation (offered to introduce to colleague or boss), Money (pre-order, LOI, payment before product exists). Absence of all three = stated interest only.
rule.rationale: Commitment signals require the user to give up something real. Stated interest costs them nothing and therefore predicts nothing.

rule.statement: Treat these responses as negative validation signals: compliments ("great idea!"), hypotheticals ("I would definitely..."), generic statements ("everyone needs this"), approval-fishing responses. If an interview produces only these, the hypothesis is not confirmed.
rule.rationale: Polite users optimize for the researcher's comfort, not for truth. Compliments and hypotheticals are the mode, not the exception, in poorly structured validation interviews.
